data.title: Kraken expands U.S. footprint with acquisition of designated contract market
data.description:
Crypto exchange Kraken acquired Small Exchange, a CFTC-licensed designated contract market ("DCM") from IG Group for $100M, giving it the ability to launch a fully U.S.-native derivatives product suite, the company said on Thursday.

"Under CFTC oversight, Kraken can now integrate clearing, risk and matching into one environment that meets the same standards as the largest exchanges in the world," said Kraken Co-CEO Arjun Sethi.

The deal is part of Kraken's larger strategy to build its global infrastructure. It now operates regulated derivatives venues in the U.K., the European Union, and the U.S. The system spans six fiat currencies and more than 450 digital and traditional assets within one architecture, Sethi said.

Earlier this year, Kraken acquired NinjaTrader, a U.S. futures platform that allows U.S.-based clients to trade CME-listed cryptocurrency futures, alongside spot crypto in a unified interface.

data.title: United Airlines expects Q4 to be its best quarter, but sees increased costs to maintain premium demand
data.description:
United Airlines’ (NASDAQ:UAL) early gains on Thursday unraveled during the company’s earnings call after executives warned that cost per available seat mile (CASM) would increase in the coming quarters to beef up its premium offering and maintain a high level of customer demand.

Shares were gaining altitude during the early part of the call after CEO Scott Kirby and COO Andrew Nocella said the carrier will realize its best quarterly revenue growth – including RASM -- in the current quarter, and that less profitable flying will be scaled back with the expectation to drive margins up a point or two each year.

Outlook:

“Fourth quarter is setting up nicely,” Kirby said on the call, adding that capacity across all three international segments is expected to outperform, even in the underperforming Atlantic segment.

Shares began to drift lower, however, when the company reaffirmed FY25 EPS guidance despite the modest revenue growth and cost efficiencies evidenced in Q3. CFO Michael Leskinen said FY25 EPS guidance would remain between $9 to $11 per share, underwhelming investors who were not only looking for a boost in profitability, but at the midpoint of $10, missed the consensus estimates of $10.86.

Additionally, Kirby warned that the flight disruptions out of Newark International Airport in Q3 could spill into Q4.

“While the impact of [Newark] has largely dissipated, we did build a small deficit in Q3 traveling into Q4,” he said.

Government Shut-down: 

"You know there hasn’t really been a measurable impact in the first couple of weeks of October though. I think the longer this drags on obviously the risk will grow on [cancellation rates and on-time performance]. So I hope our politics, politicians will figure out how to get in a room, compromise and get something done," said Kirby.

Resilient Brand Loyalty:

One segment that United executives agree is not experiencing any disruptions is the carrier’s (NASDAQ:UAL) brand loyal customers, a segment that offset any shortcomings during the quarter and remains resilient during lean times.

Consequently, the airline remains committed to catering to its brand loyal flyers. So, to increase the amenities and offerings to its premium flyers, United (NASDAQ:UAL) will continue to make investments to its offerings, expected to drive CASM ex-fuel up 2-3% during 2026.

“This is, however, profit accretive,” Leskinen assures, as the increased costs will translate into higher profits in the subsequent quarters.

Cost Control vs Revenue Growth:

Reflecting on the reported quarter,  BofA Securities’ Andrew Didora acknowledged this was not a typical quarter as cost controls drove the results, raising concerns if Q4 profitability will be driven by revenue or costs.

“RASM pressures more than offset good cost control,” agrees Deutsche Bank’s Michael Linenberg, “and we expect to see somewhat of a similar trend playing out in the December quarter (i.e. negative RASM) but with upside to EPS in the quarter,” leading Linenberg to hike his EPS target to $3.23 from $2.15.

After gaining in the early part of the session, United (UAL) shares have retreated into the red and are down by more than 5%, weighing on peers including American Airlines (AAL) and Delta Air Lines (DAL).

data.title: Equity REITs log ~23% rise in September capital raising activities - report
data.description:
Recently, equity REITs have seen a revamp in capital raising activities, with the sector reportedly raising more capital in September.

In August, U.S. equity REITs saw an ~243% rise in their capital raising activities to $7.94B from $2.31B in July. For September, equity real estate investment trusts collected $9.77B, up 23.1% month-over-month, data from S&P Global Market Intelligence showed.

However, the September 2025 figure was below the year-ago level of $10.89B, the report noted.

Below are the notable debt and equity issuances logged in September:

  • Iron Mountain (IRM) - $1.4B senior notes offering
  • Prologis (PLD) - $1.9B notes offering
  • BXP (BXP) - $1.0B senior notes offering
  • Realty Income (O) - $800.0M senior notes offering
  • Fermi (FRMI) - $784.9M common equity offering
  • Millrose Properties (MRP) - $750.0M senior notes offering
  • Service Properties Trust (SVC) - $580.2M senior notes offering
  • Public Storage (PSA) - $497.0M senior notes offering
  • Brixmor Property Group (BRX) - $400.0M senior notes offering
  • COPT Defense Properties (CDP) - $400.0M senior notes offering
  • Lamar Advertising (LAMR) - $400.0M senior notes offering
  • Diversified Healthcare Trust (DHC) - $375.0M senior notes offering
  • National Health Investors (NHI) - $350.0M senior notes offering
  • Broadstone Net Lease (BNL) - $350.0M senior notes offering
  • Brandywine Realty Trust (BDN) - $300.0M notes offering
  • American Tower (AMT) - $200M senior notes offering

data.title: Brighthouse Financial in talks with Sixth Street for $55/share sale - report
data.description:
Brighthouse Financial (NASDAQ:BHF) is in talks with Sixth Street for a sale for ~$55 a share. Brighthouse fell 1.2%.

The talks with Sixth Street come after Brighthouse came to an impasse with Aquarian Holdings late last month over the funding structure for a deal, according to a  Reuters report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

Deal talks with Sixth Street and its insurance affiliate, Talcott, were revived in recent days after Brighthouse (NASDAQ:BHF) spent weeks in exclusive talks with Aquarian, according to the report. 

Bloomberg reported last month that insurer Aquarian Holdings was in advanced discussions to pay $65 to $70 a share for Brighthouse (BHF).

No deal between Sixth Street and Brighthouse is guaranteed, and the talks could end without a deal.

Sixth Street, Brighthouse, and Talcott declined to comment to Reuters. Aquarian didn't respond to a comment request.

data.title: ManpowerGroup forecasts Q4 2025 EPS of $0.78–$0.88 as AI-driven strategies show early impact
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ManpowerGroup Inc. (MAN) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jonas Prising reported that "after 11 consecutive quarters of organic constant currency revenue declines we crossed back over to growth during the third quarter." He highlighted that stabilization in demand across North America and Europe was a key factor in this improvement. Prising noted, "we continue to see gradual broad-based signs of stabilization," with strong momentum in Latin America and APME, while Europe and North America remained below historical peaks yet stable.
  • Prising referenced the ManpowerGroup Employment Outlook Survey, which found "45% of employers planning to maintain current workforce levels, the highest since early 2022." He also stated that Manpower, the largest brand, showed revenue growth in diverse markets, and Experis began to see "early signs of stabilization in professional and IT hiring." The company also made progress in modernizing the Experis offering and integrating PowerSuite AI tools.
  • Prising added, "our ongoing modernization of the Experis offering, including enhanced consultant development and tighter integration of our PowerSuite AI tools is supporting margin improvement and future growth." He pointed to improved win rates and new enterprise programs in sectors such as financial services and life sciences.
  • CFO John McGinnis stated, "U.S. dollar reported revenues in the third quarter were impacted by foreign currency translation. And after adjusting for currency impacts, came in at the midpoint of our constant currency guidance range." He explained that system-wide revenue was $4.9 billion and reported EBITDA for the quarter was $74 million, with adjusted EBITDA at $96 million. McGinnis noted, "adjusted EBITDA margin was 2.1%."

Outlook

  • Prising said the company is monitoring "leading indicators of demand, including activity among our largest enterprise clients, new assignment starts and priority verticals such as logistics and manufacturing and year-end seasonal patterns."
  • McGinnis provided guidance for Q4 2025: "we are forecasting earnings per share for the fourth quarter to be in the range of $0.78 to $0.88." The revenue guidance range is "between a 2% decrease and a 2% increase and at the midpoint is a flat revenue trend." The guidance includes a favorable foreign currency impact of $0.08 per share and projects EBITDA margin to be flat at the midpoint compared to the prior year.

Financial Results

  • Prising reported "revenue was $4.6 billion, down 2% year-over-year in constant currency. System-wide revenue...was $4.9 billion." Reported EBITDA was $74 million, adjusted to $96 million excluding restructuring costs.
  • McGinnis said, "earnings per diluted share was $0.38 on a reported basis, while earnings per diluted share was $0.83 on an adjusted basis. Adjusted earnings per share decreased 39% year-over-year in constant currency."
  • Gross profit margin for the quarter was 16.6%. McGinnis detailed, "staffing margin contributed a 40 basis point reduction due to mix shifts towards enterprise accounts. Permanent recruitment activity was softer than expected, and the lower contribution resulted in a 20 basis point decline."
  • The Manpower brand grew 3% in organic constant currency, Experis declined 7%, and Talent Solutions declined 8% year-over-year. In the Americas, revenue increased 6% year-over-year in constant currency. Southern Europe saw a 1% decrease, Northern Europe a 6% decline, and APME an 8% increase. Free cash flow was $45 million for the quarter.

Q&A

  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: Asked about early-cycle pickup in staffing volumes and gross margin dynamics. Prising replied, "it's like a frozen labor market. There's very little hiring going on, and there's very little workforce reductions going on...we're starting to see a distinct stabilization and growth in Manpower." McGinnis confirmed that gross margin pressure is "primarily mix shift towards enterprise clients."
  • Kartik Mehta, Northcoast Research: Inquired about volatility in major markets and gross margin drivers. McGinnis explained, "France...saw improvement in the trend during the course of the third quarter...Italy...saw an improving trend." On gross margin, he said enterprise mix "is the main driver of what's happening on the staffing side."
  • John Ronan Kennedy, Barclays: Asked about demand indicators and AI implementation. Prising indicated stabilization is strongest in Europe and the U.S., with AI "now driving measurable gains with approximately 30% of new client revenue derived from AI rated probability."
  • Mark Marcon, Robert W. Baird: Questioned gross margin in key markets and ability to stimulate convenience market. Prising replied, "business mix shift being the main driver of the staffing margin, not any price competition."
  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked about France's business tax and blue-collar vs. white-collar staffing. McGinnis called it "too early to tell" on business tax. Prising tied Experis softness to client investment in AI over traditional IT.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a neutral to slightly negative tone, pressing on margin pressure, market volatility, and the impact of political and economic uncertainty, but responded positively to stabilization and AI-driven progress.
  • Management's tone during prepared remarks was cautiously optimistic, emphasizing stabilization, signs of growth, and strategic progress. In Q&A, responses were measured, with confidence expressed in operational improvements and technology investments, but some defensiveness when questioned about margin and market risks ("As I said before..." and "we believe...").
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed slightly more confidence due to early signs of stabilization and growth, but concerns about macroeconomic uncertainty and margin persisted.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q4 EPS was raised to $0.78–$0.88 compared to the Q3 guidance range of $0.77–$0.87 in the previous quarter, with revenue guidance shifting from a possible 4% decrease to a range of -2% to +2%.
  • Management highlighted crossing back to organic constant currency revenue growth after 11 quarters of decline, a notable shift from the prior focus on stabilization.
  • Margin pressures intensified due to enterprise client mix, while AI impact and operational efficiency initiatives gained prominence.
  • Analysts maintained focus on margin, market volatility, and macro trends, but also probed deeper into AI-driven growth and digital transformation.
  • Management's confidence in technology investments and signs of stabilization increased relative to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, particularly in Europe and North America, as key risks.
  • Margin pressure from enterprise client mix and softer permanent recruitment was highlighted.
  • Analysts flagged volatility in key markets, political uncertainty in France, and the possibility of continued business tax surcharges.
  • Management is mitigating risks through cost containment, organizational simplification, and accelerated technology and AI initiatives.

Final Takeaway

ManpowerGroup leadership presented a cautiously optimistic outlook, citing a return to organic constant currency revenue growth, ongoing stabilization in North America and Europe, and early measurable impact from AI investments. Despite ongoing margin pressures and macroeconomic uncertainties, the company is prioritizing operational efficiency, digital transformation, and targeted growth initiatives. Guidance for Q4 2025 EPS was set at $0.78–$0.88, with revenue expected to stabilize or modestly increase, as management aims to leverage its AI-enabled platform for future margin expansion and market share gains.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Banner outlines mid-single-digit loan growth guidance amid robust deposit gains and dividend increase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Banner Corporation (BANR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Mark J. Grescovich, President and CEO, announced, "Banner Corporation reported a net profit available to common shareholders of $53.5 million or $1.54 per diluted share for the quarter ended September 30, 2025." He highlighted the company's "strategy to maintain a moderate risk profile" and noted the "strength of our balance sheet, coupled with the strong reputation we maintain in our markets will allow us to manage through the current market uncertainty." Grescovich emphasized the company's "core earnings power," citing pretax pre-provision earnings of $67.8 million and core revenue of $169 million. He also noted a return on average assets of 1.3% and said, "core deposits continue to represent 89% of total deposits." Grescovich announced a "4% increase in the core dividend to $0.50 per common share" and referenced positive recognition from Forbes, Newsweek, J.D. Power, and S&P Global Market Intelligence.
  • Jill Rice, Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer, reported that "overall credit metrics remained strong. Delinquent loans improved slightly and now represents 0.39% of total loans, down 2 basis points from the linked quarter." She highlighted that "adversely classified loans declined by $16 million quarter-over-quarter" and stated, "portfolio loan balances, while basically flat when compared to the linked quarter, remained up 4% year-over-year." Rice affirmed, "we still anticipate reporting a mid-single-digit growth rate for the full year."
  • Robert Butterfield, CFO, stated, "We reported $1.54 per diluted share for the third quarter compared to $1.31 per diluted share for the prior quarter. The $0.23 increase in earnings per share was primarily due to an increase in net interest income, a lower provision for credit losses, as well as the current quarter, including a gain of $1.4 million on the disposal of assets." Butterfield noted, "Deposits increased by $489 million during the quarter, primarily due to core deposits increasing $426 million." He added, "Banner repurchased 250,000 shares during the quarter and announced a 4% increase in its declared dividend to common shareholders."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated expectations of a "mid-single-digit growth rate for the full year" in loans, as stated by Rice. Butterfield discussed margin expectations: "If the Fed does 1 rate cut in the quarter, then we're expecting that our margin would be relatively flat... and under that scenario where we have essentially multiple rate cuts in 1 quarter, then we would expect some margin compression in that quarter."
  • Grescovich indicated, "we're very proud of our performance and how we are viewing the future of performance for Banner."

Financial Results

  • Banner reported net profit available to common shareholders of $53.5 million or $1.54 per diluted share for the third quarter. Core earnings were $67.8 million and core operations revenue was $169 million. Pretax pre-provision income increased 8.5% to $67.8 million compared to the prior quarter, and revenue from core operations increased $6 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • Core deposits increased by $426 million, and total deposits increased by $489 million in the quarter. Loan-to-deposit ratio ended at 84%. Net interest income increased by $5.6 million from the prior quarter, with a tax equivalent net interest margin of 3.98%.
  • The company recorded a gain of $1.4 million on asset disposals and a $377,000 net gain on security sales. The allowance for credit losses stood at $159.7 million, providing 1.36% coverage of total loans.

Q&A

  • Jeff Rulis, D.A. Davidson & Co.: Asked about timing of FHLB payoffs and margin impacts. Butterfield responded, "we started to see the deposits come in fairly early in the quarter... probably halfway through the quarter. So there could be a little bit of additional benefit there from just an overall funding cost standpoint." On future margin impacts from rate cuts, Butterfield explained, "we would expect some margin compression in that quarter. So moderate in nature, what we would expect because we will have those adjustable rate loans that are 29% of our portfolio."
  • Rulis queried buyback activity and M&A appetite. Grescovich replied, "we thought we were confident in repurchasing shares at an average rate of $63.50... the company's earnings momentum is very strong... there's some momentum with M&A and I would think that if an opportunity presented itself, we would certainly be in a position to combine and do some additional fill-in acquisitions."
  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Inquired on buyback criteria and deposit growth drivers. Butterfield said, "we're always looking at the different alternatives for capital deployment... the share repurchase authorization in place... ultimately, it's going to be based on an assessment of the market conditions."
  • David Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about competitive landscape and loan originations. Rice stated, "the competitive landscape is it's not really unchanged... the decline in originations this quarter was multifaceted... our pipelines have built and are continuing to build."
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Sought details on deposit pricing and margin modeling. Butterfield indicated, "we're modeling a 28% deposit beta... our expectation is that we would get a similar deposit beta that we got last time."
  • Tim Coffey, Janney: Questioned credit monitoring and loan-to-deposit ratio. Rice explained, "the vast majority, and I'm talking north of 95% of our loans have personal or corporate guarantees." On loan-to-deposit ratio, "if we were to approach 95% would be okay, I don't think we'd go above that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was largely constructive, focusing on margin sustainability, capital deployment, competitive positioning, and deposit growth, but included probing on margin compression and buyback logic.
  • Management displayed confidence in prepared remarks and responses, using language such as "we're very proud of our performance" and "we continue to build capital," while acknowledging headwinds from potential margin compression and signaling flexibility in capital deployment. During Q&A, management tone was measured, providing detailed explanations and reiterating guidance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained inquisitive, with slightly more focus on margin outlook and capital deployment, while management maintained a consistent level of confidence and detailed responses.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Net profit per share increased from $1.31 in Q2 to $1.54 in Q3, with reported core earnings and revenue from core operations both higher than the prior quarter.
  • Core deposit growth rebounded after a Q2 decline, while loan growth was flat quarter-over-quarter but up year-over-year.
  • Guidance language on mid-single-digit loan growth was reiterated, with management more explicitly discussing capital deployment options, including increased share buybacks and a higher dividend.
  • Management's confidence remained steady, but with enhanced emphasis on flexible capital allocation and M&A optionality. Analyst questioning shifted to a greater focus on margin sustainability amid anticipated rate cuts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted expectations of margin compression if multiple Fed rate cuts occur in a quarter, with Butterfield stating, "we would expect some margin compression in that quarter."
  • Rice highlighted ongoing competition and the need to remain disciplined in underwriting, especially in the face of requests for longer interest-only periods.
  • The company faces continued exposure to variable-rate loan repricing and industry competition, but management emphasized robust capital, liquidity, and risk management practices.
  • Analysts expressed concern about sustainability of deposit growth and the impact of rate cuts on net interest margin.

Final Takeaway

Banner Corporation's third quarter 2025 results showcased growth in core earnings, robust deposit inflows, and disciplined expense management, resulting in an increased dividend and active share repurchase program. Management reiterated its guidance for mid-single-digit loan growth for the year and signaled readiness to deploy capital through buybacks, dividends, or opportunistic M&A, while maintaining a moderate risk profile and strong credit metrics. The company remains confident in its ability to navigate margin pressures and capitalize on growth opportunities, supported by a loyal core deposit base and continued investment in technology and talent.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Electronic Arts' Battlefield 6 smashes records for the series
data.description:
Electronic Arts (NASDAQ:EA) sold more than seven million copies of its new Battlefield 6 title in the first three days of release, a record for the first-person shooter franchise. For reference, the Battlefield V video game has sold just over 14 million copies to date since its release in 2018.

Battlefield 6 came out on October 10 to largely positive reviews, which was a relief to the company after the previous two games were commercial disappointments. Electronic  Arts (NASDAQ:EA) said the game had over 172 million matches played online and accumulated over 15 million hours watched on streaming services during the prior three-day weekend, as well as earning the most concurrent players for Battlefield ever.

"Battlefield 6 marks a return to form for the series. Destructible, dynamic environments, a huge variety in multiplayer modes, and its trademark All-Out Warfare mode are just some of the highlights that make the game so much fun," summarized PC Magazine about the release.

The game features an expansive multiplayer mode of up to 128 players and single-player campaigns. Battlefield 6 was released on PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X|S, and PC (Steam, EA App, Epic Games Store).

Shares of Electronic Arts (EA) are swapping hands just below $201, which is a discount to the $210 per share private equity buyout price.

data.title: Coatue’s ‘Fantastic 40’ – Stocks to lead in AI
data.description:
Investment management firm Coatue updated their “Fantastic 40” list.

This is a curated “growth and innovation index” list that spotlights 40 companies believed to be best positioned to “lead in an AI- and technology-driven world,” according to the firm’s platform.

The curated list is gathered from the top 150 public and private technology companies that have demonstrated to be compounders of growth and a five-year CAGR below market. This list also excludes companies that are facing multiple compression, slowing revenue growth, revenue down revision, EPS down revision, “or not being viewed as having AI growth potential,” the firm said.

New additions include Google (GOOG), (GOOGL), SAP (SAP), ServiceNow (NOW), AMD (AMD), SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF), Constellation (CEG), and Databricks.

Here’s the refreshed full list:

data.title: Praxis surges after late-stage trial success for movement disorder therapy
data.description:
Praxis Precision Medicines (NASDAQ:PRAX) continued to climb on Thursday, adding more than 200% in the afternoon session as Wall Street welcomed a late-stage trial success for lixacaltamide, its experimental therapy for essential tremor, the commonest type of movement disorder.

Based on data from its Phase 3 Essential3 program, which comprises two simultaneously enrolled trials, Praxis (PRAX) said that the eight-week Study 1 hit the primary endpoint related to the change in a clinical measure known as mADL11.

According to topline data from Study 1, ulixacaltamide led to a statistically significant and clinically meaningful 4.3-point average improvement in the mADL11 score at Week 8 and achieved all secondary endpoints with statistical significance.

Study 2, which enrolled those who improved by 3 points in the mADL11, also hit the primary endpoint, as 55% of patients on ulixacaltamide maintained response compared to 33% in the placebo arm, with a statistically significant effect.

As for safety, Praxis (PRAX) noted that its experimental therapy was well-tolerated and added that there were no mortalities or treatment-related serious adverse events.

The company expects to submit a new drug application to the FDA in early 2026 to secure marketing approval for ulixacaltamide and request a pre-NDA meeting ahead of the filing.

Guggenheim and Jefferies were among the brokerages to boost their price targets on Praxis (PRAX) after the readout. With a Buy recommendation on PRAX, Guggenheim analyst Yatin Suneja lifted his price target to $350 from $155 per share, assuming an 80% probability of success for the drug’s approval.

Noting investor concerns regarding ulixacaltamide’s sales profile and high discontinuation rates, the analyst pointed to Essential3’s home-based trial design and argued that tolerability could improve in a commercial setup.

Meanwhile, with a Buy recommendation, Jefferies also raised its price target on PRAX to $300 from $65, increasing the drug’s probability of success for approval to 70% from 0% previously.

Update 1.00 PM ET: Adds latest stock move and comments from analysts

data.title: Vale nixes plan to build $231M Quebec nickel sulfate facility
data.description:
Vale (NYSE:VALE) said Thursday it has ended plans to build a C$325 million (US$231 million) nickel sulfate facility in Quebec after sole client General Motors (GM) pulled back its push to build electric vehicles due to weaker than expected demand and U.S. policy changes that make EVs less attractive.

GM (GM) will not immediately need nickel sulfate in Quebec because it has changed the timing of its Ultium Cam electric vehicle battery venture in Bécancour with South Korean steelmaker Posco (PKX), a Vale (NYSE:VALE) spokesperson told Mining.com, and "as a result, Vale Base Metals is cancelling its nickel sulphate plant project."

Vale’s (VALE) project would have been the first-of-its-kind fully domestic nickel sulfate facility for the North American market.

Vale (VALE), which has nickel mines in Ontario, Manitoba and Newfoundland and Labrador, as well as processing facilities, said it will continue to work with GM (GM) on their partnership to supply Canadian nickel into the automaker's North American supply chain.
